concept.title: Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
concept.shortdesc: For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

section.title: Why reuse is the “real reason” many teams adopt DITA
section.p: For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:
But reuse only works when it’s designed, governed, and validated. This topic explains the core reuse mechanisms and how to use them without creating maintenance pain.

section.title: Reuse types: topic reuse vs content reuse vs reference reuse
section.p: You reuse a whole topic by including it in multiple maps. This is the cleanest form:

ul.li: a “Safety overview” concept used in multiple manuals,
a “Reset password” task reused across product editions,
a “CLI options” reference topic used in both admin and developer docs.

section.p: You reuse parts of topics:
This is powerful but can create complexity if overused.
You reuse references (links, product names, terms) using keys so the same topic can adapt to different contexts without editing.

section.title: Conref: direct content reuse
section.p: conref allows an element to pull content from another location. Think of it as “include this snippet here.”
This pulls the element with id="install-note" from the topic with id="common-notes" in common-notes.dita.

p.b: When conref is a great choice

ul.li: when the reused content depends on local context,
when authors “fix” shared snippets without coordinating,
when the referenced snippet changes structure often,
when the conref chain becomes too deep to understand.

p.b: Conref governance patterns (what professional teams do)

ul.li: Create a reuse/ folder (or CCMS equivalent).
Assign ownership to a small group.
Use review requirements for changes to shared snippets.

section.p: Pattern B: “Reusable API” approach Treat each shared snippet like an API:
Pattern C: Shallow reuse Avoid deep conref chains:

ul.li: don’t conref a snippet that contains conrefs that contain conrefs…
shallow reuse is easier to debug and maintain.

section.title: Keys and keyref: the late-binding superpower
section.p: Keys are map-defined labels that resolve to:
A topic uses keyref to point to a key, and the actual target is resolved based on the map at build time.

p.b: Why this matters in real life

section.p: Keys enable:

ul.li: reuse of topics across multiple deliverables without editing.

p.b: Example: product name and support URL

section.p: Change the key definition in the map, and the same topic publishes differently—without editing the topic.

li.b: Use keys for anything that varies by deliverable

section.p: - product name, - URLs, - partner branding, - company names, - legal disclaimers by region (sometimes).
- Treat key names like public identifiers.
- who can change keys, - how changes are reviewed.

section.title: Conkeyref: content reuse through keys
section.p: conkeyref lets you pull content via a key instead of a direct file#id reference. This is powerful when:

ul.li: the source of reusable content varies by deliverable,
the same topic must pull different snippets depending on map context.

section.p: You publish the same “Install” topic for:
Each needs a slightly different legal notice. Instead of editing the topic or maintaining three variants, you define:
This is advanced, but it scales well when managed correctly.
Step 1 — Define the key in each map to point to a different snippet source:
section.codeblock:
<!-- product-a.ditamap --> <keydef keys="legal-install-note" href="reuse/product-a-legal.dita"/>
<!-- product-b.ditamap --> <keydef keys="legal-install-note" href="reuse/product-b-legal.dita"/>
section.p: Step 2 — Create the snippet sources with matching IDs:
section.codeblock: <!-- reuse/product-a-legal.dita --> <topic id="legal-notices"> <title>Legal</title> <body> <note id="install-legal"> Product A is licensed under the Acme Standard License. See https://acme.example.com/license-a for details. </note> </body> </topic>

<!-- reuse/product-b-legal.dita --> <topic id="legal-notices"> <title>Legal</title> <body> <note id="install-legal"> Product B is licensed under the Acme Enterprise License. Contact your account manager for terms. </note> </body> </topic>

p.b: Step 3 — Use conkeyref in the shared topic:

section.codeblock: <!-- topics/install.dita (shared across both maps) --> <task id="install-task"> <title>Installation</title> <taskbody> <prereq> <note conkeyref="legal-install-note/install-legal"/> </prereq> <!-- ... steps ... --> </taskbody> </task>
section.p: When you build with product-a.ditamap, the conkeyref resolves to the Product A legal note. Build with product-b.ditamap, and the same topic automatically shows the Product B text — no editing required.

p.b: When conkeyref is worth the effort

ul.li: product families with shared content but different key spaces.

section.title: Filtering and variant management: one source, many outputs
section.p: DITA filtering is often called profiling or conditional processing.
- platform="windows" - audience="admin" - product="pro"

ol.li: Your build rules include/exclude based on those attributes.

section.p: Don’t use everything Pick only the dimensions you truly need. Each added dimension multiplies complexity.
If authors invent values freely, filtering becomes unreliable:
Professional programs define allowed values and enforce them.
Many DITA ecosystems use subject scheme maps to:
Even if you don’t implement subject scheme immediately, write down your vocabulary and enforce it in reviews.

section.title: Designing reusable and filterable content without chaos
section.p: Here are strategies that prevent “variant spaghetti.”

p.b: Strategy A: Separate by topic when variants are large

section.p: If Windows and Linux procedures differ significantly:
Use filtering only for minor differences, not completely different workflows.

p.b: Strategy B: Use filtering for small differences only

section.p: Filtering works well for:

ul.li: a note that applies only to one platform.

section.p: If half the topic is conditional, split the topic.

p.b: Strategy C: Keep conditional blocks coarse

section.p: Instead of tagging every sentence:
This reduces maintenance.

p.b: Strategy D: Combine keys + filtering intelligently

section.p: Keys handle “context changes” (names/links). Filtering handles “content inclusion changes” (platform-specific steps). Use both; don’t force one mechanism to do everything.

section.title: Reuse + translation: how to save money and time
section.p: Reusable content reduces translation effort only if:

ul.li: you avoid minor rewrites in shared snippets.
Avoid embedding variable product names in shared snippets; use keys.
Don’t conditionalize tiny phrases across many languages—use topic-level variants when needed.

section.title: Validation: how you keep reuse and variants reliable
section.p: A DITA program needs automated checks:

ul.li: build warnings treated as failures in CI.
Build must complete with zero unresolved references.
Key space must resolve without ambiguity.
Filtering must produce non-empty outputs for required deliverables.
Link checking must pass for internal references (external link checking is optional, but useful).

section.title: A realistic “reuse plan” for a growing doc set
section.p: Start simple:
- Build a library of reusable concepts and tasks.
- Add a reuse/common-notes.dita library.
- Keep topics portable across maps.
- Choose 1–2 dimensions; don’t overcomplicate.
Then scale:

ul.li: formalize subject schemes and vocabulary enforcement.

section.title: Common reuse and variant mistakes
section.p: Fix: prefer topic reuse; conref only stable, context-independent snippets.
Fix: use topic splitting; conditionalize only where differences are small.
Fix: define allowed values and enforce them.

p.b: Mistake: Hard-coded product names and URLs

section.p: Fix: use keys and keyref.
Fix: assign owners and add review rules.

section.title: Analytics and Instrumentation
section.p: To measure the impact of reuse, instrument your output with analytics. Track conref and keyref usage, and monitor which topics are reused most. Use analytics dashboards to identify content that could benefit from further reuse or consolidation.

section.title: Personalization and Conditional Processing
section.p: Use DITA conditional attributes (such as audience, product, platform) and DITAVAL files to personalize content for different users or outputs. Example:

section.title: Integrating with Conversational AI and NLP
section.p: Expose this topic's content to chatbots or virtual assistants by making it available via API or structured export. Use semantic markup and metadata to help NLP systems extract relevant answers for user queries.

section.title: Continuous Improvement and Feedback Loops
section.p: Add feedback widgets to your published documentation and use analytics to monitor user suggestions. Regularly review feedback to refine reuse strategies and update content for clarity and effectiveness.

section.title: What's next?
section.p: You can now write typed topics, build projects, and apply reuse patterns. In Part 4: Information Architecture, you will learn how to organize topics at scale — designing map hierarchies, defining taxonomy and controlled vocabulary, and structuring content so it can grow from 10 topics to 1,000 without losing consistency.
